factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies Molasses Act (1733) with Parliamentary citation 6 Geo. II c.13 or equivalent official format
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly identifies the Molasses Act (1733) and gives its formal Parliamentary citation as “6 George II c. 13,” which is equivalent to the required “6 Geo. II c.13” format.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explicitly states Molasses Act regulates trade/customs duties on molasses, sugar, rum and does NOT classify enslaved people as property or use chattel language
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly describes the Molasses Act as imposing customs duties on molasses, sugar, and rum and emphasizes it is a trade regulation measure. It does not use chattel/property language to describe enslaved people in the Molasses Act section, reserving such language for separate slave-code discussions.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies 1732 Colonial Debts Act or Debt Recovery Act (5 Geo. II c.7) treating enslaved people as seizable property for creditor debt collection
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses the 1733 Molasses Act and various colonial slave codes, but it never mentions the 1732 Colonial Debts Act/Debt Recovery Act (5 Geo. II c.7) or identifies its treatment of enslaved people as seizable property for creditor debt collection. There is no statutory citation or description of creditor seizure provisions tied to that 1732 Act.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Cites Barbados 1661 slave code characterizing enslaved people as "goods and chattels" or equivalent explicit property terminology
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly cites the Barbados Slave Code (1661) as classifying enslaved Africans as “goods and chattels,” including the quoted line about protecting them as “men's other goods and Chattels.”

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains Barbados law designated enslaved people as chattels for debt payment AND as real estate for inheritance or other legal purposes
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response says the Barbados Slave Code classified enslaved people as “goods and chattels,” but it does not explain the additional legal treatment of enslaved people as real estate for inheritance or other purposes. There is no discussion of the dual classification (chattels for debt payment vs. realty for inheritance) in Barbados law.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Jamaica adopted or adapted Barbados slave code framework in 1664/1684 period with 1717-1730s updates or consolidations
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response says Jamaica’s slave laws were derived from the Barbados model, but it does not state that Jamaica adopted/adapted the Barbados slave code framework specifically in the 1664/1684 period, nor does it mention 1717–1730s updates or consolidations. Those dates and the adoption/consolidation claim are absent.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes Jamaica law toggled or shifted between characterizing enslaved people as chattel property and real property depending on legal context
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response says Jamaica’s laws “defined enslaved individuals as property and chattels,” but it does not note any legal toggling between treating enslaved people as chattel (personal property) versus real property depending on context. There is no discussion of shifting characterization by forum (e.g., inheritance, execution, or land law contexts).

factual-accuracy.requirement: Connects Stono Rebellion (September 1739) to passage of South Carolina's comprehensive 1740 Negro Act or slave code
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that South Carolina’s 1740 slave code was passed immediately following the Stono Rebellion (1739), directly linking the rebellion to the comprehensive 1740 Negro Act/slave code.

factual-accuracy.requirement: References Yorke-Talbot Opinion (1729) indicating enslaved people brought to England did not become free, reinforcing metropolitan property understanding
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response does not mention the Yorke–Talbot Opinion (1729) at all, nor does it state its holding that enslaved people brought to England did not become free or connect it to metropolitan property understandings. It discusses general ambiguity of English law and later Somerset (1772) instead, omitting the required 1729 reference.

factual-accuracy.requirement: References authoritative printed statute collections (Hall's Barbados Acts 1643-1762, McCord's South Carolina Statutes, Jamaica Acts of Assembly 1681-1737, or equivalent)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response does not reference authoritative printed statute collections like Hall’s Barbados Acts, McCord’s South Carolina Statutes, or Jamaica Acts of Assembly 1681–1737 (or an equivalent statutes-at-large volume). Instead it cites web sources (e.g., Statutes Project, Wikipedia, UNB site, National Archives guide) without naming any printed statute compilation.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Specifies which sources contain explicit chattel language (colonial codes, 1732 Act) vs indirect support (Molasses Act)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response distinguishes that the Barbados and South Carolina slave codes contain explicit “goods and chattels/chattels personal” language, while stating the Molasses Act focuses on trade duties and does not explicitly regulate enslaved people, only indirectly supporting the slave economy.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explains specific economic chain linking Molasses Act to slavery (molasses→rum→slave trade or rum-slaves-molasses triangle)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response notes that New England’s rum industry relied on cheap molasses and mentions the broader link between molasses production and slavery, but it does not lay out the specific chain (molasses→rum→slave trade or the rum–slaves–molasses triangle). It lacks an explicit step-by-step explanation connecting the Molasses Act to the triangular trade mechanism.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Warns against citing Molasses Act for property definitions, directing to colonial codes or 1732 Act for chattel language
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response discusses the Molasses Act as a trade regulation statute and notes it did not explicitly regulate enslaved populations, but it does not warn against citing the Molasses Act for property/chattel definitions or direct the user to colonial codes or a 1732 Act for chattel language.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Analyzes chattel-for-debts vs real-estate-for-inheritance as functional strategy serving distinct legal purposes, not contradiction
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response does not discuss chattel-for-debts versus real-estate-for-inheritance as a functional legal strategy, nor does it frame those as distinct purposes rather than a contradiction. It focuses on the Molasses Act and slave codes, without addressing debt satisfaction via chattels or inheritance via real property. The required comparative analysis is absent.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Synthesizes how Parliamentary trade/debt law and colonial control codes functioned together as integrated imperial slavery system
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response discusses Parliament’s Molasses Act as trade/debt-style regulation and contrasts it with colonial slave codes, then explicitly links trade regulation to the plantation economy “inseparably linked” to slavery and describes both as parts of a broader system sustaining slavery. This amounts to a synthesis of how metropolitan trade law and colonial control codes functioned together as an integrated imperial slavery system.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies 1732 Debt Recovery Act as Parliamentary assertion of metropolitan power over colonial private law or empire-wide harmonization
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response discusses the 1733 Molasses Act and various colonial slave codes, but it never mentions the 1732 Debt Recovery Act or characterizes it as a Parliamentary assertion of metropolitan power over colonial private law or as empire-wide harmonization. There is no analysis of debt recovery legislation or its imperial constitutional significance. The required identification is absent.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Lists at least 4 specific colonial surveillance/control mechanisms (passes, patrols, assembly restrictions, literacy limits, punishments)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response lists multiple specific control mechanisms, including bans on free movement without written passes, patrol/capture of escaped slaves, restrictions on assembly/communication implied via movement controls, literacy prohibitions, and punishments such as whipping, mutilation, branding, and death.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Claims the Molasses Act (1733) contains explicit chattel, property, or slave-status language classifying enslaved people
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly says the Molasses Act “did not explicitly regulate enslaved populations” and was chiefly a trade regulation law, and it provides only trade-duty excerpts from the Act. It does not claim the Act contains chattel/property/slave-status language about enslaved people.

presentation-quality.requirement: Clearly distinguishes primary statutory/archival sources from secondary peer-reviewed scholarly analysis throughout response
presentation-quality.explanation: The response does not clearly and consistently distinguish primary statutory/archival sources (e.g., original acts, statutes-at-large, archival records) from secondary peer-reviewed scholarship; it mixes quotations, summaries, and bracketed “Sources” without labeling which are primary vs secondary. It also cites non-peer-reviewed/tertiary items (Wikipedia, YouTube, Britannica, educational PDFs) alongside purported scholarship without clarifying their status.

presentation-quality.requirement: Provides pinpoint quoted excerpts anchored to reliable primary-source editions with act/regnal year/section/page locations, avoiding long verbatim dumps
presentation-quality.explanation: The response includes some short quoted snippets, but it does not anchor them to reliable primary-source editions with precise act/regnal year/section/page (or equivalent) locations. Several “sources” are secondary or unreliable (e.g., Wikipedia, YouTube, general websites), and it lacks pinpoint citations for the quoted slave-code language. It also contains long paraphrased summaries rather than properly located primary excerpts.

presentation-quality.requirement: States that verbatim transcription from 18th-century statutes is error-prone and offers verification workflow with specific scan/edition/page guidance
presentation-quality.explanation: The response does not warn that verbatim transcription from 18th-century statutes is error-prone, nor does it provide a verification workflow. It lacks specific guidance such as which scans/editions to use and how to cite or cross-check by page/folio numbers.

presentation-quality.requirement: Structures response explicitly by the 4 requested deliverables (statutory texts, context, articles, debate)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is explicitly organized into four numbered sections matching the requested deliverables: statutory texts (Molasses Act and slave laws), contextual comparison of parliamentary vs colonial codes, recent articles, and the scholarly debate about the Molasses Act’s purpose.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses formal academic language without contractions, casual expressions, or conversational asides
presentation-quality.explanation: The response consistently uses formal, academic-style phrasing and avoids contractions such as “don't” or “it's.” It also avoids casual expressions and conversational asides, maintaining a report-like tone throughout.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses conversational phrases like "here's a rundown" or "this one gets talked about" or second-person address inappropriate for scholarly research
presentation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly uses second-person and conversational framing, e.g., “tailored for historical novel authenticity” and “aiding precise and authentic novel writing,” which is not strictly scholarly in tone.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses excessive wordiness, filler phrases, or unnecessary elaboration that extends length without adding substantive historical or legal information
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is highly verbose, with long introductions, repeated summaries, and extensive contextual paragraphs that add length without providing additional statutory quotations or precise legal citations. It includes multiple overview sections and concluding restatements that function as filler relative to the user’s targeted request for specific statutory language and recent peer-reviewed articles.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites at least 2 of Hall's Barbados Acts 1643-1762, McCord's South Carolina Statutes at Large, Jamaica Acts of Assembly 1681-1737, Parliamentary Statutes at Large, Baskett prints, or Trott's Laws
citation-quality.explanation: The response does not cite any of the specified compilations (Hall’s Barbados Acts 1643–1762, McCord’s South Carolina Statutes at Large, Jamaica Acts of Assembly 1681–1737, Parliamentary Statutes at Large, Baskett prints, or Trott’s Laws). It references items like Wikipedia, Britannica, and a “Statutes Project,” but none of the required sources appear, so it cannot meet the “at least 2” requirement.

citation-quality.requirement: References relevant UK National Archives Colonial Office series by colony (e.g., Barbados Acts: CO 30; Jamaica correspondence: CO 137; Jamaica Acts: CO 139) and/or provides catalogue search guidance for locating colonial statutes/acts
citation-quality.explanation: The response mentions The National Archives UK in general terms but does not reference specific Colonial Office (CO) series numbers by colony (e.g., CO 30, CO 137, CO 139) or give concrete catalogue-search guidance for locating colonial statutes/acts within those CO series.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites South Carolina 1740 Negro Act defining enslaved people as "chattels personal" or using legal terminology (deemed/held/taken/reputed/adjudged in law)
citation-quality.explanation: The response discusses the South Carolina Slave Code of 1740 and quotes language about “chattels personal,” but it does not cite the South Carolina 1740 Negro Act by name nor provide the specific statutory phrasing using the legal terms (deemed/held/taken/reputed/adjudged in law). It also does not reference it as the “1740 Negro Act” explicitly.

citation-quality.requirement: Identifies where primary sources can be found (statute compilations, colonial printed acts, archival collections) and gives how-to-locate guidance (such as catalog search terms, series/collection names, etc)
citation-quality.explanation: The response points to places to find primary texts (e.g., The National Archives UK research guide and a “Statutes Project” link for the Molasses Act, plus a full-text South Carolina slave code site). It also gives some locating guidance by naming specific acts/codes and citations (e.g., “6 George II c. 13”), though detailed catalog search terms and archival series names are limited.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites at least 3 verifiable peer-reviewed works published within 2015-2025
citation-quality.explanation: The response lists several items as “peer-reviewed” but does not provide at least three clearly verifiable peer-reviewed works from 2015–2025 with complete, checkable citations (authors, year, title, journal/book). Some sources are non-peer-reviewed (e.g., Wikipedia, YouTube, Britannica, educational PDFs), and the peer-review status/date of others is not verifiable from the provided references.

citation-quality.requirement: For each article cited, includes author, title, journal, year, volume/issue, pages, and DOI or stable URL; labels uncertainty if present
citation-quality.explanation: The response lists sources mostly as bare URLs and does not provide full article-level citations with author, journal, year, volume/issue, page range, and DOI/stable URL for each cited work. Several items are books, educational resources, Wikipedia/YouTube, or unspecified articles, and no uncertainty labeling is applied to incomplete bibliographic details.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Wikipedia, popular history websites, or non-peer-reviewed sources as authoritative evidence for statutory language or legal interpretation
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites Wikipedia and other popular/non-peer-reviewed sources as evidence, including a Wikipedia link for the Barbados Slave Code and Stono Rebellion, plus Britannica, History Guild, and an OSU lesson plan, in its “Sources” section while discussing statutory language and legal interpretation.
